query: evidence that viral co-infection increases severity in children with acute respiratory infections

retrieved_docs.title: Respiratory Syncytial Virus in Adults -- Pathophysiology -- Respiratory Syncytial Virus and Comorbid Conditions
retrieved_docs.content: RSV lower respiratory tract infection can be exacerbated by coinfection with bacterial pneumonia, particularly involving Streptococcus pneumoniae . [4] The pathophysiology underlying this association is not fully established and likely includes inflammatory loss of respiratory tract epithelium and localized immune dysfunction. A recent study's results suggest that RSV enhances susceptibility to bacterial infection by attenuating the antibacterial protective immunity of alveolar macrophages. [32]

retrieved_docs.title: Severe Acute Respiratory Syndrome -- Prognosis
retrieved_docs.content: Prognostic features identified by epidemiologic studies for poor outcomes include diabetes, chronic hepatitis B and other underlying comorbid conditions, older age, non-typical presentation, and an elevated serum lactate dehydrogenase (LDH) at the time of admission. [7] Diabetes mellitus and heart disease are especially notable among comorbid conditions as prognostic factors for a worse outcome. [7] A high viral load at presentation has also been independently associated with a worse prognosis. [7] Age is a very important prognostic factor. Patients older than 65 show the highest rates of mortality, exceeding 50%. [7] Children less than 12 years of age generally have uneventful courses and good outcomes. [7]

retrieved_docs.title: Respiratory Syncytial Virus Infection in Children -- Enhancing Healthcare Team Outcomes -- Outcomes
retrieved_docs.content: The majority of children with RSV have an excellent outcome. Even those who need admission are usually discharged in several days. However, high-risk infants with other co-morbidities may require longer admission and some may even require mechanical ventilation. The overall mortality for RSV is less than 1%, and in the United States, there are less than 400 deaths attributed to RSV each year. Infants with congenital heart disease, prematurity or chronic lung disease tend to have the highest mortality. Further infants who are immunocompromised also tend to have longer admissions compared to normal infants. In the long run, some infants with RSV may develop wheezing, but this is debatable. Recent studies do not show an increased risk of asthma. [15] [16] [Level 5]

retrieved_docs.title: Respiratory Syncytial Virus Infection in Children -- Continuing Education Activity
retrieved_docs.content: The human respiratory syncytial virus (RSV) is one of the most common viruses to infect children worldwide and increasingly is recognized as an important pathogen in adults, especially the elderly. The most common clinical scenario encountered in RSV infection is an upper respiratory infection, but RSV commonly presents in young children as bronchiolitis, a lower respiratory tract illness with small airway obstruction, and can rarely progress to pneumonia, respiratory failure, apnea, and death. This activity reviews the pathophysiology of respiratory syncytial virus infection and highlights the role of the interprofessional team in its management.

retrieved_docs.title: Respiratory Syncytial Virus in Adults -- Pathophysiology -- Respiratory Syncytial Virus and Comorbid Conditions
retrieved_docs.content: As seen in other causes of respiratory tract infections in older adults, RSV infection can lead to acute cognitive and functional decline, with a prolonged impact on activities of daily living. The pathogenic mechanism behind this is not yet well understood, but it appears to share many similarities with other post-viral fatigue conditions. [29] Not surprisingly, functional impairment due to RSV infection in older adults occurs more frequently in those with underlying cardiopulmonary disease. [30] Similar immunosenescence associated with advanced age can predispose individuals to more severe illnesses through diminished T-cell responses. [31]

retrieved_docs.title: Respiratory Syncytial Virus Infection in Children -- Pearls and Other Issues
retrieved_docs.content: There is evidence that severe RSV infection and hospitalization for RSV early in life raise the risk for the development of recurrent wheezing, childhood asthma, and allergic sensitization. Moreover, there is some evidence that palivizumab prophylaxis may decrease the incidence of later recurrent wheezing in treated subjects. However, the extent, duration, and mechanism of a link between RSV and asthma has not been fully elucidated and remains a subject of vigorous research.

retrieved_docs.title: Parainfluenza Virus -- Epidemiology
retrieved_docs.content: HPIVs are one of the main causes of hospitalization in children less than 5 years of age due to acute respiratory tract infection, with a rate accounting for up to 17% of hospitalizations. [5] Serological surveys have demonstrated that 60% of children infected with HPIV-3 are around 2 years of age, whereas for children around 4 years of age, the rate of HPIV-3 infection increases to up to 80%. [6] Co-infections of HPIV and other respiratory viruses are frequent and may cause a more serious, severe, and prolonged disease course. [5]

retrieved_docs.title: Respiratory Syncytial Virus in Adults -- Pathophysiology -- Respiratory Syncytial Virus and Comorbid Conditions
retrieved_docs.content: Infection with RSV appears to be associated with the accelerated development of coronary artery disease and congestive heart failure. The association between RSV infection and cardiovascular disease is similar to that of other infectious causes of pulmonary infections, where the underlying pathophysiology is thought to be a combination of right ventricular strain and inflammation-associated plaque destabilization. RSV has been found within the myocardium, where it may contribute to direct tissue injury. Moreover, in those with preexisting coronary artery disease, the physiologic stress of infection due to fever and hypoxemia can cause demand ischemia. [28]

retrieved_docs.title: Upper Respiratory Tract Infections With Focus on The Common Cold -- Introduction
retrieved_docs.content: The common cold is a syndrome that manifests as a mild-to-moderate, self-limited URTI, characterized by inflammation that causes malaise, nasal congestion, rhinorrhea, sneezing, pharyngitis, cough, and occasional fever and headache. Various other respiratory viruses and bacteria, mentioned here but not presented in detail, can cause common cold symptoms that overlap with those due to rhinovirus. Please see StatPearls' companion resources, " Pharyngitis ," " Acute Laryngitis ," " Acute Sinusitis ," " Recurrent Acute Rhinosinusitis ," " Acute Otitis Media ," " Mastoiditis ," " Influenza ," " Parainfluenza Virus ," " Respiratory Syncytial Virus Infection in Children ," " Adenoviruses ," " Human Metapneumovirus ," and " Features Evaluation and Treatment of Coronavirus COVID19 ," for further information on these pathogenic etiologies.

retrieved_docs.title: Respiratory Syncytial Virus Infection in Children -- Continuing Education Activity
retrieved_docs.content: Objectives: Describe the pathophysiology of respiratory syncytial virus infection. Review the presentation of respiratory syncytial virus infection. Outline the treatment and management options available for the respiratory syncytial virus. Describe interprofessional team strategies for improving care coordination and communication to advance the treatment of respiratory syncytial virus and improve outcomes. Access free multiple choice questions on this topic.

retrieved_docs.title: Respiratory Syncytial Virus Infection in Children -- Differential Diagnosis
retrieved_docs.content: Asthma Bronchiolitis Influenza Croup Bronchitis Pneumonia

retrieved_docs.title: Respiratory Syncytial Virus Infection in Children -- Epidemiology
retrieved_docs.content: RSV is a widespread pathogen of humans, due in part to the lack of long-term immunity after infection, making reinfection frequent. It infects 90% of children within the first 2 years of life and frequently reinfects older children and adults. The majority of patients with RSV will have an upper respiratory illness, but a significant minority will develop lower respiratory tract illness, predominantly in the form of bronchiolitis. Children under the age of one year are especially likely to develop lower respiratory involvement, with up to 40% of primary infections resulting in bronchiolitis. Worldwide, it is estimated that RSV is responsible for approximately 33 million lower respiratory tract illnesses, three million hospitalizations, and up to 199,000 childhood deaths; the majority of deaths are in resource-limited countries. There is seasonal variation in RSV incidence, but seasonal effects vary with worldwide geography; temperate climates have a marked winter-spring predominance, and tropical and equatorial climates may have less pronounced spikes with the more interseasonal disease. Morbidity and mortality are significantly higher in a subset of patients, including premature infants, patients with preexisting cardiac, pulmonary, neurologic, and immunosuppressive disorders, and the elderly. [6] [7]

retrieved_docs.title: Upper Respiratory Tract Infections With Focus on The Common Cold -- Epidemiology
retrieved_docs.content: The incidence of the common cold in the general adult population is between 2 to 5 episodes per year. Children represent the reservoir of rhinovirus, and the incidence rate of infection is approximately 4-fold higher than that of adults, likely due to child-to-child contact at daycare and school settings. [9]  Consistent with the transmission mechanisms of respiratory viruses, a direct correlation between crowding and the risk of developing a URTI is present. A correlation between URTIs and the quality of indoor ventilation is not apparent. [10]  Exposure to cold air, originally thought to be the harbinger of the syndrome that carries its name, does not predispose individuals to catching the common cold.

retrieved_docs.content: Supplemental oxygen when indicated Airway augmentation as appropriate Monitoring of and replacement of any fluid deficits Symptomatic control of temperature and cough Rest to reduce oxygen demand Treatment of any comorbidities and/or concomitant bacterial pneumonia The concepts of diagnosis, prevalence, clinical role, and treatment of viral pneumonia are in flux for several reasons. 1. There is a growing population at increased risk of viral pneumonia:

retrieved_docs.title: Viral Pneumonia -- Enhancing Healthcare Team Outcomes -- Outcomes
retrieved_docs.content: The outcomes in most healthy people with viral pneumonia are excellent. However, in individuals who are immunocompromised or at extremes of age, the prognosis is guarded. Several adenovirus serotypes are known to cause severe pneumonia leading to bronchiectasis and irreversible atelectasis. It is estimated that anywhere from 10%-40% of children may suffer some irreversible lung damage after adenovirus pneumonia. Viral pneumonia in a patient with an underlying disease can add morbidity and lead to marked hypoxia. Overall, most patients recover with supportive measures and have no residual sequelae. [17] [18] (Level 5)

retrieved_docs.title: Acute Bronchitis -- Etiology
retrieved_docs.content: Influenza, RSV, and human coronaviruses typically exhibit peak incidence during winter. As stated previously, this seasonal pattern arises as people tend to spend more time indoors and engage in close interpersonal contact, creating an environment conducive to the rapid transmission of these viruses. Rhinoviruses typically experience peaks in incidence during the spring and fall seasons, coinciding with shifts in weather patterns. Seasonal allergies may also contribute to these peaks by potentially facilitating the transmission of the virus during these times. Enteroviruses, a group encompassing several viruses responsible for respiratory and gastrointestinal illnesses, peak in summer. This case surge aligns with increased outdoor activities, providing more significant opportunities for viral transmission.

retrieved_docs.title: UMLS -- Run 1 -- Level 2 -- Community 775
retrieved_docs.content: Acute bronchitis is classified under the broader category of acute inflammation (الْتِهابِ شعبي) and is clinically associated with pulmonary inflammation (الْتِهاب رِئَوِيّ), indicating that patients with acute bronchitis may develop or present with features of pneumonia. Influenza (الإنفلونزا) is linked to pulmonary inflammation as well; although it is not a form of pneumonia itself, influenza can precipitate pneumonia, including secondary bacterial pneumonia caused by cocci (التهاب رئوي بالعقديات). This relationship is reflected in the hierarchical view that influenza has a child concept of pneumonia with cocci, emphasizing the risk of bacterial superinfection following viral illness. Neonatal pneumonia (التهاب رئوي وليدي) is recognized as a specific subtype of pulmonary inflammation, further illustrating the spectrum of pneumonia presentations across age groups. Zanamivir, a member of the class of neuraminidase inhibitors and direct‑acting antivirals, is effective in preventing influenza infection, highlighting its role in antiviral prophylaxis. In the treatment of bacterial pneumonia caused by cocci, the fluoroquinolone antibiotic gemifloxacin is employed, demonstrating a therapeutic option for this specific type of secondary pneumonia. Overall, the interconnections among acute bronchitis, influenza, and various forms of pneumonia underscore the importance of recognizing viral‑bacterial interactions, appropriate preventive measures such as neuraminidase inhibitor prophylaxis, and targeted antimicrobial therapy for bacterial complications.

retrieved_docs.title: UMLS -- Run 7 -- Level 1 -- Community 47
retrieved_docs.content: Within the respiratory disease hierarchy, influenza is inversely related to pulmonary inflammation, is a child condition of fungal‑type pneumonia, and serves as the parent condition of the broader syndrome termed “الإنفلونزا”. The neuraminidase inhibitor zanamivir, a direct‑acting antiviral, provides prophylaxis against “الإنفلونزا”. These relationships delineate the connections between specific antimicrobial and antiviral agents and their target respiratory infections, emphasize important drug‑drug interaction warnings, and clarify the hierarchical taxonomy of acute bronchitis, various pneumonia subtypes, and influenza, thereby supporting informed prescribing and patient safety.

retrieved_docs.title: UMLS -- Run 2 -- Level 3 -- Community 1431
retrieved_docs.content: Influenza, referred to in Arabic as الالتهاب الرئوي, is a viral respiratory infection that can lead to or be associated with pneumonia. The broader term الالتهاب الرئوي also includes specific forms such as التهاب رئوي وليدي, which denotes neonatal pneumonia, a distinct subtype of pneumonia occurring in newborn infants. Understanding that influenza may precipitate pneumonia and that neonatal pneumonia is a particular manifestation of pneumonia aids clinicians in identifying at‑risk patients, especially infants, and in applying appropriate preventive and therapeutic strategies.

retrieved_docs.content: Among antivirals, neuraminidase inhibitors such as zanamivir belong to the direct‑acting antiviral subclass and are used for influenza prophylaxis. retrieved_docs.title: UMLS -- Run 19 -- Level 3 -- Community 1552
retrieved_docs.content: Respiratory distress syndrome is classified as a type of اضطراب لدى حديث الولادة (neonatal disorder). The broader category اضطراب لدى حديث الولادة includes the specific conditions كثرة الأرومات الحمر الجنينية and رضح الولادة. Virologie is an allowed qualifier for اضطراب لدى حديث الولادة, for respiratory distress syndrome, and also for كثرة الأرومات الحمر الجنينية and رضح الولادة, indicating that viral factors may be relevant in the assessment and management of these neonatal disorders. This hierarchical organization links respiratory distress syndrome to other neonatal conditions and emphasizes the potential role of virology across the spectrum of neonatal pathology.

retrieved_docs.content: (English) is the parent term for its Arabic equivalent (الإنفلونزا) and for coccidioidal pneumonia as a child condition. These hierarchical and mechanistic relationships guide therapeutic selection and patient‑care decisions across viral and fungal diseases.

retrieved_docs.title: UMLS -- Run 26 -- Level 2 -- Community 695
retrieved_docs.content: Acute bronchitis is classified as a subtype of الْتِهابِ شعبي and is clinically associated with الْتِهاب رِئَوِيّ (pulmonary inflammation). Influenza is regarded as a form of الْتِهاب رِئَوِيّ, and it is also listed as a child of التهاب رئوي بالعقديات (coccal pneumonia), reflecting that viral influenza can predispose to or coexist with bacterial pulmonary infection. The pediatric condition التهاب رئوي وليدي (neonatal pulmonary inflammation) is a child of الْتِهاب رِئَوِيّ, indicating it is a specific neonatal variant of pulmonary inflammation. Gemifloxacin may be used to treat التهاب رئوي بالعقديات, highlighting its role as an antibiotic effective against coccal pneumonia. Recognizing these hierarchical and clinical relationships helps clinicians understand how acute bronchitis, influenza, and various forms of pneumonia are interconnected and guides appropriate therapeutic decisions, such as employing gemifloxacin for bacterial lung infections while being aware of the potential for viral infections like influenza to complicate or trigger secondary bacterial pulmonary disease.

retrieved_docs.title: UMLS -- Run 10 -- Level 3 -- Community 1771
retrieved_docs.content: Symptoms are closely related to and may be considered synonymous with the broader category of signs and symptoms of ill‑defined conditions. In this hierarchical framework, diseases and injuries are classified as having the parent category of signs and symptoms of ill‑defined conditions, indicating that they are understood through the manifestation of such nonspecific signs. A specific example of a child element within this category is الانهيار في الدورة الدموية (circulatory collapse), which represents a distinct clinical manifestation that can arise from ill‑defined conditions. Together, these relationships highlight that the identification of symptoms and signs, including serious events like circulatory collapse, is essential for recognizing and managing diseases and injuries that present without clear, well‑defined diagnostic criteria.

These contraindicated class relationships underscore the risk of adverse cardiac effects and other drug‑interaction hazards when fluoroquinolones or related agents are co‑prescribed. Disease hierarchy relationships further inform clinical reasoning: prostatitis is categorized under inflammatory conditions of male genital organs; pneumonia is a child condition of bacterial pneumonia, which is transmissible (přenos); influenza serves as a parent disease for both viral influenza and for secondary bacterial (cocci‑associated) pneumonia, illustrating the potential progression from viral to bacterial lung infection. This integrated understanding of quinolone agents, their indications, contraindications and the hierarchical connections among related infectious diseases supports informed selection, dosing, monitoring and safety management in patient care.

retrieved_docs.title: UMLS -- Run 0 -- Level 3 -- Community 1826
retrieved_docs.content: The category “signs and symptoms of ill‑defined conditions” is presented as a parent concept that includes the specific child entity “غشي.” In this framework, the general term “Symptoms” is described as closely related to, and potentially synonymous with, the broader phrase “signs and symptoms of ill‑defined conditions,” indicating that the two expressions refer to overlapping clinical manifestations. Consequently, the specific manifestation “غشي” is understood to fall under the umbrella of nonspecific or ill‑defined symptomatology, and clinicians should recognize its inclusion within this broader symptom category when evaluating and documenting patient presentations.
